items.mistake: Removing all IQR outliers before knowing whether they are errors or signal
items.fix: Inspect flagged rows, use domain rules, and reserve removal for confirmed errors; for fraud or failure prediction, keep extremes or label them explicitly.
items.why: Statistical rarity and business-critical events often coincide; blanket deletion biases the model toward the average case.
items.example: Dropping every transaction above Q3 + 1.5×IQR removes most fraud positives in a card dataset where fraud amounts are intentionally large.

items.mistake: Computing IQR or z-score thresholds on the entire dataset including test data
items.fix: Fit quartiles, means, and percentiles on the training split only; apply the same numeric bounds to validation and test.
items.why: Test-set statistics leak future information into preprocessing, inflating offline scores.
items.example: If test contains a $10M house, including it when setting the 99th percentile clip bound lets the model indirectly see test distribution during prep.

items.mistake: Applying z-scores to heavy-tailed or skewed features without transformation
items.fix: Plot the distribution first; use IQR, log transform, or MAD-based scores when data is skewed or multi-modal.
items.why: Mean and standard deviation are pulled by the tail, so z-scores mis-rank typical points and under-flag true extremes.
items.example: Income data with a long right tail: |z| > 3 may flag only mid-range earners while billionaires look 'normal' after shifting μ.

items.mistake: Clipping targets and features with the same bounds without thinking about units
items.fix: Set bounds per column from that column's training distribution; document whether bounds are IQR fences or percentiles.
items.why: A single global cap across heterogeneous columns distorts some features and leaves others unprotected.
items.example: Clipping both `age` and `annual_spend` at the 1st/99th percentile of a combined vector is wrong—compute quantiles per feature.
